ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for open-face sandwich with ham, egg, and avocado. FOODON_00002473 (sandwich food product) used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FJP (Sandwiches) is the closest parent category; no specific open-face variant with these toppings exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226559009 (Sandwich) used as closest parent; no specific open-face ham-egg-avocado sandwich concept exists.

serving.description: 1 open-face sandwich (1 slice of bread with toppings)
serving.natural_language_portion: One open-face sandwich consisting of one thick slice of toasted bread (approximately 60 g) topped with sliced ham (approximately 50 g), one fried or poached egg (approximately 60 g), and sliced avocado (approximately 50 g), totalling approximately 220 g per serving.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — composite estimation from SR Legacy entries: Bread (toasted white/sourdough), Ham (cured, sliced), Egg (whole, cooked), Avocado (raw)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite café dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted-ingredient composition using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries: Bread white toasted (FDC 325871), Ham cured sliced (FDC 174608), Egg whole cooked fried (FDC 173424), Avocado raw (FDC 171705). Proportions: bread 27%, ham 23%, egg 27%, avocado 23% by weight. Per-100g values derived from weighted average of these four components.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; analogous estimate based on mixed sandwich with egg, ham, and avocado on white/sourdough bread
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific open-face sandwich. GI estimated at 52 based on analogous mixed sandwich entries from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021). White bread GI ~70 is moderated substantially by the fat and protein content of egg, ham, and avocado, which slow gastric emptying and reduce postprandial glucose response. Avocado contributes monounsaturated fat and fibre, further lowering the composite GI. Composite GI of approximately 52 (low category, ≤55) is defensible. GL per serving = (52 × 15.4 × 2.20) / 100 = 17.6 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from cured ham
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit consumption of this sandwich due to its high sodium content (approximately 480 mg per 100 g, equating to ~1,056 mg per serving), primarily from cured ham; choosing low-sodium ham or reducing portion size is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load with low GI moderated by fat and protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: This sandwich has a low estimated GI of approximately 52, moderated by the fat from avocado and protein from egg and ham, making it a relatively suitable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when portion size is controlled to one serving (approximately 220 g, providing ~34 g carbohydrate).

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from egg and ham balanced by heart-healthy monounsaturated fat from avocado
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Avocado provides beneficial monounsaturated fatty acids (~5.8 g per 100 g) that support cardiovascular health, but the saturated fat from egg yolk and cured ham (~2.6 g per 100 g) and the high sodium from ham warrant moderation for individuals with cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Ham, Egg and Avocado Open-Face Sandwich is a Western-style café breakfast or brunch dish served at specialty coffee bars in Singapore, such as EC Coffee Bar on Carpenter Street. It consists of a single thick slice of toasted bread — typically sourdough or white toast — topped with sliced cured ham, a cooked egg (fried or poached), and fresh sliced avocado. The dish is open-faced, meaning it uses only one slice of bread rather than two, making it lighter in carbohydrate content than a conventional sandwich. The flavour profile is savoury and rich, with the creamy, buttery texture of avocado complementing the saltiness of the ham and the richness of the egg yolk. Nutritionally, this dish provides a balanced macronutrient profile per serving: approximately 436 kcal, 21.6 g protein, 24.6 g fat (with a favourable proportion of monounsaturated fat from avocado), and 33.9 g carbohydrate. The avocado contributes dietary fibre (~2.4 g per 100 g) and heart-healthy monounsaturated fatty acids. The primary nutritional concern is the sodium content from cured ham, which is elevated at approximately 480 mg per 100 g. The estimated glycemic index is approximately 52 (low category), making it a relatively suitable option for blood glucose management when portion-controlled. This dish is not halal due to the pork-derived ham. It is a popular café brunch item in Singapore's specialty coffee scene and is representative of the Western café food trend in the city-state.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this specific composite café dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted-ingredient composition using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for the four main components (bread, ham, egg, avocado). GI is an analogous estimate based on comparable mixed sandwich entries from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level USDA data is high quality and the ingredient proportions are reasonable for a standard café serving.
notes: 1. Food group corrected from input 'Dairy and eggs' to 'Mixed', as this is a composite sandwich dish containing bread, ham, egg, and avocado. 2. Cuisine corrected from 'Malaysian' to 'Western / Café'; the dish is a Western-style café item served at EC Coffee Bar in Singapore. Multilingual names provided per Singaporean dish rules (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi). 3. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data; no direct approved-source entry exists for this dish. 4. GI is an analogous estimate (GI ~52, low) based on University of Sydney GI Database mixed sandwich analogues; no direct GI measurement exists for this specific dish. 5. Sodium is elevated due to cured ham; this is the primary clinical concern. 6. The dish contains pork (ham) and is therefore not halal. 7. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, and all micronutrients are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data and are approximate. 8. Price from input (SGD 20.70) is preserved as context but not included in the nutrient record per Veritas scope. 9. All -1 sentinel values have been replaced with estimated numeric values from USDA component data; no -1 values remain in per_100g.
